findings.description: Inverted expiry check: 'age < self.ttl' returns True for FRESH entries, so the cache deletes fresh L1 entries (line 54) and serves stale ones forever — the comparison must be 'age >= self.ttl'.
pickle.loads on bytes read from Redis is a remote-code-execution sink — anyone who can write to (or MITM) the cache store achieves arbitrary code execution on deserialization.
RedisClient.setex silently ignores ttl_seconds, so the L2 store never expires; the TTL contract the class advertises is unenforced and callers relying on L2 expiry are misled.
The L1 dict (_local) has no size bound or eviction policy beyond the (broken) per-key TTL, so a long-lived process with diverse keys leaks memory until OOM.
No thread safety: the check-then-del at lines 53-54 and the read-modify-write of stats/_local race under concurrent access, which a dict-in-front-of-Redis cache invites.
Hit/miss accounting is asymmetric: an expired-L1 then L2-miss performs two lookups but records a single miss, and L2 promotions count as hits, so exported stats are only approximate.
stats.local_entries is written eagerly in set() but export_stats() recomputes len(self._local) and ignores the stored field, leaving dead/divergent state with no single source of truth.
set() updates stats.local_entries but warm() and invalidate() do not, so the field is inconsistently maintained across the write paths even apart from the export_stats divergence.
